segments.text: Good afternoon, ladies and gentlemen.
Each Saturday afternoon at this time,
a national broadcasting company brings to the war telescope from London.
You will hear NBC's observer in the British Capitol, John McVain,
an interesting discussion of developments of the war.
So now to London to see what Mr. McVain has in store for us.
Come in, John McVain and London.
This is John McVain and London.
As the hour and the day of invasion come closer,
signs of increased tension are becoming more visible in Britain.
The people are living on two planes.
visible in Britain. The people are living on two planes. One is the round of daily work.
On that plane, nerves become irritated more quickly than usual. People do their jobs,
work hard at them, but always with the feeling that everything from now on will be a kind of
mocking time until the day when the assault begins. You might compare it to what a boxer does
with his time on the day of a championship fight. He's done his training. Nothing will be really
important now until he jumps from his chair under the arc lights when the bell sounds for the
first round. Meanwhile, he must spend the day going through the motions of eating, sleeping,
and diverting himself, but always he knows that life will again seem important to him, only when
the second step out of the ring can he stand spacing his opponent with his whole future at stake.
For the people of Britain, life on the second plane consisted looking forward to the coming
operations, wondering about them speculating, hoping confident that when the attack begins
daily life will again take on significance in me.
I think it's partly from this feeling of suspended existence that it's been responsible
for the wave of strikes in Britain.
Bus drivers and conductors in London declare their jangled nerves can't stand more trips
per day on the summer schedule, so they strike for a few days and drift back to work.
company employees on strike in Manchester, leave housewives with gas cookers
facing the prospect of no hot meals over the weekend. The coal strikes in recent
weeks, the reached serious proportions. The series of strikes can't be condoned at
such a time and isn't by any important body of opinion in Britain. But the fact
that they do occur is one manifestation of invasion nerves. From a certain amount
of knowledge of the British, I guess that when operations do begin there'll be
no strikes in this country.
Invasion is a descendable in the government's methods
of dealing with the problem.
Government spokesmen have been in fine
to blame it all on the sinister activities
of a few hundred British Trotskyites.
At present, there's a great hue and cry about them.
For a day or two, played up in the London papers
on strictly thriller lines, a new defense regulation
drawn up with the consent of the trade unions
has been aimed at the Trotskyite agitators.
Yet this is all a crude simplification,
the surface attempt at dealing with a deep problem.
The Trotskyites are undoubtedly guilty of doing as much as they can against the
bore effort.
But their efforts would be unavailing if second front nerves in the fact that
real grievances had been allowed by the government to trail along for years
without settlement didn't create the conditions favourable to such unofficial
strikes and stoppages.
Invasion nerves have shown up even in royal quarters here, only thus can be
explained the statement I heard young keen Peter of Yugoslavia make this week to
the effect that all the aid the Allies had sent his people
wouldn't suffice to equip even one battalion.
I can't recollect any time in history
in which a monarch cooked his own goose
so quickly in a single sentence.
Peter evidently met only me high-loving in his sibs
when he referred to my people.
Well, thousands of tons of weapons and equipment
have been sent to martial detail.
It was the first time in public then
that the king proclaimed himself as only the king
of me high-loving is dwindling force
instead of the king of all Yugoslavs.
In fact, if you were betting on the future of the Yugoslav monarchy, something more than
a good outside chance might be given to the possibility of a tie-up between Tito and
the monarchy in the person of one of King Peter's younger brothers.
But even though Britain can give examples of all nerves, constructive work is being done
that will help international understanding and cooperation.
I heard Representative Fulbright yesterday explain the work that the Allied Educational Reconstruction
group is doing. Mr. Fulbright believes that in the cooperation of bodies like UNRA and
his committee are being laid, the foundations of political cooperation in the post-war years.
The education group will rebuild the wreckage of education in the occupied countries, get
classes going, provide teacher training, textbooks, paper, pencils, to make possible training
for citizenship of children of our lives after they've been liberated. Such schemes are
hopeful for cooperation possible in such varied types of activity as the
second front education and yesterday's statement about international currency
should point the way to cooperation and other fields after the war is over. On
these war telescope broadcasts, you usually hear men with some special
knowledge discuss some particular phase of the war. Today I'm going to do
something new. Introduce two of the men who'll be part of NBC's European
invasion team. They've just arrived in London from New York, Washington, and their first impressions
of life in Britain at this crucial moment would perhaps be your impressions if you'd had
the chance to make the trip. You'll hear both of these men many times in coming months.
One you've heard often in the past year broadcasting from New York. He is W.W. Chaplin, veteran
foreign correspondent, and author of the book, 70,000 miles of war. He's been to Russia,
near in Cairo, but this is the first time he's been to Britain since September 1939,
the first month of the war. The voice of the second will be new to you. He's NBC's
Walker correspondent accredited to the U.S. Navy, George Wheeler. He's been a broadcasting
executive in Washington, and this is his first trip to Britain in wartime. Now to begin,
what do you fellas get as a first impression of Britain in these pre-second front days?
You find it's different from September 39 Bill. Well, of course, John, the most obvious
difference is the number of Americans here. It's a little hard for me to realize even
yet that this is England. Because there are so many Americans here. That's the first
impression I had, the first change indexed. Well, the next change I noticed was the result
of the bombing, and that came to me only gradually. I got to London that night during an alert
incidentally and drove to a hotel through blackout streets deserted except for
people who have very special and very important duties at such times.
Next morning there seemed to be very few changes the city as a whole is just the
same the barrage balloons still look like great grace nails climbing slowly up
the wall of the sky and off of the old landmarks to give an impression of
business as usual. It was quite suddenly that it came to me that big gaps in
rows of buildings didn't indicate new construction. Planned and prepared for by
the intentional removal of old buildings. This was bomb damage. The debris
neatly swept away. When I was here last no bomb had yet fallen on London. As I've
walked around the streets in the past couple of days my eyes have already become
educated there are many changes in London does that job with your impression George yes it does John
from just a quick look you might get the impression that the bombing wasn't as severe as you'd
supposed but taking into account the ability to rebuild and repair the surest indication of the
beating that London has taken is the absence of window glass many many blocks have their sightless
For reminders, whereas in the other respects, they may appear completely untouched.
Well, what do you think of the most outstanding characteristics of life and London at the moment?
Well, I've been struck by the contrast between the very old and the very new tradition
existing beside advanced economic and sociological thinking,
final buildings beside modern architecture.
The thing that struck me first and the hardest is the general feeling of calm confidence.
It wasn't that way, in 39. It wasn't that way at all.
War had just been declared.
No one knew what was going to happen in any next minute.
But they were quite ready to believe it would be something awful.
Children were being evacuated from the big cities.
Everyone was carrying gas masks at all times.
Even waiters in restaurants wore them,
strapped on behind like a bustle while waiting on tables.
Well, it isn't like that now.
After all, this home front has been a very active war front for a long time.
You mean people don't have to wonder about what sort of things may happen?
That's it, John. They've been through everything that can happen to a home front
and they know all the answers.
So, they just go on about their business, calmly, confidently, completely unafraid.
To me, it's, well, it's more than reassuring. It's really inspiring.
Do you think this British attitude has had its effect on all our people here?
I think it must have had.
Competence and calm are just as contagious, just as catching as our hysteria, bewilderment
and the jitters of any kind.
Certainly even the little time I've been here has been long enough to show very plainly
that our folks here aren't suffering from any kind of jitters.
And I think the British attitudes must have been a big help to them when they were getting settled in.
I've been noticing a lot of little things.
For instance, a notice in the papers,
proposing a share of the ride scheme for taxi cabs.
That's a little amazing, you know,
because we've been doing that in many American cities
for a long time.
And I think rationing is much more complete here than at home.
The ordinary man doesn't seem to be as affected
by inflation as his counterpart in the States.
As long as he keeps living an ordinary life, he's all right.
It's when he wants to kick up his heel, but he feels the pinch.
But even the ordinary man has to pay 50 cents
a pack for his cigarettes, George.
And I think you'll get a shark when you go to the movies
and have to fork over a couple of dollars a seat.
But Bill, were conditions here any different
from what you expected?
Yes, I worked on.
I'd expect it to see things generally.
It seems a lot more than they are.
I thought the people would be changed more
by what they've gone through.
I thought all the physical hardships.
the law of taxation, the rationing, all that sort of thing.
I thought it would show more on the surface.
Actually, it hardly shows at all.
On the surface.
What's your first impression of the American army over here, Bill?
Well, of course, I really haven't had a chance
to see much of it.
Nothing of it in the field.
But the judge, just from what you can see in London,
I'd say that our army here is an army ready to go.
Certainly the morale is tops.
And I'm told that a degree, the perfection of training, is just as high.
You came over with a Navy George. What's that angle?
Well, I got a wonderful tick out of the way men who are once bankers and butchers and beer salesmen have pitched in with the regulars.
As an American, it made me pretty proud to see the way they're doing a terrifically complicated business of running convoys and fighting ships.
Any incidents you remember in particular?
Well, I can't say much, of course, but there was one story that I think is worth telling.
It was the story of a colored mess attendant.
He used to spend all his spare time studying up on plain recognition, silhouettes and that sort of thing.
One of the officers heard about him and decided to give him a test.
The final exam for experts consisted of identifying silhouettes,
shown on a screen in a darkened room for only a 50th of a second.
This colored mess attendant quietly identified 100 out of 100.
They set the machine to 100th of a second per flash.
The mess attendant's average fell to 95 out of 100.
Then half apologetically, he proceeded to give the nationalities
and general types of the remaining five planes.
And now when the bosons whistle sounds all hands to general quarters,
that slight shy steward's mate is not a mess attendant anymore.
He's assistant to the gunnery officer for the whole ship pretty good. He's the lucky man to have eyes like that
Speaking of ships. What do you hope to do covering the Navy in the invasion George?
Well, I hope to get wherever and whatever there is of naval action and recorded on the spot John at sea
I've been experimenting with the up-to-the-minute recording device. We hope to rush these
recordings away and broadcast them home as quickly as as humanly possible. What about your trip over here, Bill?
So your first sight of Britain, an American air base, that sort of thing.
Well, John, I'm really not sure just how much I can say about things like that yet.
You always have to find out the ground rules in each new theater.
But certainly I can say that I came by air in one of the big planes of our air transport
command, and that it was a very nice trip, not so much as a bump all the way from Washington
to Wells and to the place we landed somewhere in the United Kingdom.
asked about an American airbase. The only one I've seen so far is that place we landed.
And I guess all I can say about that is that it was impressive, very impressive. If everything
over here is as smooth as that airbase. And I understand it is. Well, then I can understand
why the German radio is having such a case of high temperature invasion jitters as it
is right now. And I shouldn't be surprised if not the high temperature got higher still
before it's over. Thank you, Bill Chaplin and George Wheeler. This is John McVeigh returning
you to the NBC Newsroom in New York. And so ends another of NBC's War Telescope broadcast,
a regular weekly presentation from the NBC Newsroom in New York. During today's program, you'll
heard John McVeigh and NBC's Observer in London presenting WWW Chaplin and George Wheeler,
BC's newly arrived observers in London. This is the National Broadcasting Company.
